culturalNote: Kilig is the delicious, giddy feeling you get when something romantic happens — a brush of the hand, a sweet text, a love confession in a teleserye. It's not just happiness; it's that electric flutter in your chest, the involuntary smile, the shriek you share with your friends. Filipinos don't just feel kilig — they perform it, squirming in their seats and grabbing whoever is next to them.

Diskarte is the Filipino art of making things work with whatever you have. It's not just being clever — it's a survival skill, a hustle, a creative way of navigating life when the system doesn't work in your favor. When someone has diskarte, they can fix a broken engine with wire and tape, negotiate their way out of a traffic ticket, or turn a small loan into a thriving business. It's admired because it reflects resilience.

english: the urge to squeeze something cute
culturalNote: Gigil is the overwhelming urge to pinch, squeeze, or even gently bite something unbearably cute — a chubby baby's cheeks, a tiny puppy, a toddler in a miniature barong. It can also describe the trembling frustration you feel when you're holding back anger. The word captures a physical tension that English can only describe in a full sentence. When a lola sees her apo for the first time, she is consumed by gigil.

english: come what may / leave it to fate
culturalNote: Bahala na is the Filipino way of surrendering to the universe — part faith, part courage, part fatalism. It literally invokes 'Bathala' (the old word for God), meaning 'God will take care of it.' Filipinos say it before exams they didn't study for, before jumping off cliffs into the sea, before proposing to someone way out of their league. It's not laziness — it's a deep trust that things will work out, combined with the bravery to act despite uncertainty.

Utang na loob is a deeply felt sense of obligation and gratitude that comes from receiving a significant favor or kindness. It's not a simple 'thank you' — it's a lifelong bond. If someone helped your family during hard times, you carry that debt in your heart forever. It shapes Filipino relationships, politics, and even career choices. Some say it's the glue that holds Filipino communities together; others say it can be a chain that binds too tightly.

Pakikisama is the art of maintaining smooth interpersonal relationships and group harmony, even at the cost of your own preferences. It means going along with the group, not making waves, and always considering how your actions affect others. If your barkada wants to eat at Jollibee but you want McDo, you go to Jollibee without complaining. It's valued because it keeps communities tight-knit, but it can also make it hard to say no or stand out.

english: withdrawal of affection as expression of hurt
culturalNote: Tampo is not exactly sulking, not exactly the silent treatment, and not exactly a tantrum — it's a uniquely Filipino emotional withdrawal that signals hurt without confrontation. When someone has tampo, they become quiet, distant, and a little cold, waiting for the other person to notice and make amends. It's considered a softer alternative to direct anger and is deeply embedded in Filipino relational culture. The remedy is 'lambing' — extra affection and attention until the hurt is soothed.

Pasalubong is the expected gift you bring home whenever you travel anywhere — even just to the next town. It's not optional; coming home empty-handed is practically a social crime. The word comes from 'salubong' (to meet/greet), because the gift is what greets your loved ones when you return. It can be anything from dried mangoes to a keychain to a whole lechon. The act of giving pasalubong reinforces family bonds and says 'I was thinking of you while I was away.'

Lihi refers to the Filipino belief that a pregnant woman's intense cravings, experiences, and even the things she stares at during early pregnancy can influence the appearance and personality of her unborn child. If a mother craves mangoes, the baby will have yellowish skin. If she stares at a handsome actor on TV, the baby will be good-looking. It's a deeply held folk belief that persists across generations, blending superstition with the joy and anxiety of expecting a child.

english: just because / no further explanation needed
culturalNote: Basta is the ultimate conversation-ender and the most Filipino way to avoid explaining yourself. 'Bakit?' 'Basta.' It can mean 'just because,' 'as long as,' 'the point is,' or 'I don't want to explain anymore.' Parents use it to end arguments with children. Friends use it to justify questionable decisions. It's simultaneously frustrating and liberating — a single word that shuts down all need for logic or reasoning and asks you to simply accept what is.
